question: what is biodynamic farming examples
title: What Is Biodynamic Farming? Real Examples and What Makes It Different
metaDescription: Biodynamic farming takes soil health, composting, and biodiversity further than organic — treating the farm as a living organism. Here are real examples of what that looks like in practice.
content:
# What Is Biodynamic Farming? Real Examples and What Makes It Different

When I first encountered biodynamic farming, I'll be honest, some of it made me raise an eyebrow. Lunar calendars for planting. Herbal preparations stuffed into cow horns and buried in the ground. A philosophy that treats the farm as a cosmic organism interconnected with planetary forces. That's a long way from how most people think about growing food.

But here's the thing I've learned: underneath the more esoteric elements, biodynamic farming is built on a foundation of soil biology that is completely aligned with everything Albert Howard figured out, everything Gabe Brown is doing in North Dakota, everything that regenerative agriculture points toward. The biology is sound. The insistence that the farm is a living system — not a factory — is absolutely right. And the outcomes on well-run biodynamic operations are genuinely impressive.

Let me give you a real understanding of what biodynamic farming is, and some concrete examples of what it looks like.

## Where Biodynamics Came From

Biodynamic agriculture was developed by Rudolf Steiner, an Austrian philosopher and scientist. In 1924, a group of farmers came to him with a problem. They'd noticed that their soil was deteriorating, their crops were losing vitality, and their animals were becoming less healthy. They'd been farming with what were then

methods — early chemical inputs, monoculture approaches — and they could see the results were not good.

Steiner gave a series of eight lectures that became the foundation of biodynamic practice. He was ahead of his time in insisting that the farm was a living organism, not a machine, and that soil health was the foundation of plant health and human health. Those ideas are now well-supported by decades of soil biology research, even if the scientific community arrived at them through a very different path.

The Biodynamic Association describes the core of Steiner's approach as this: a farm should generate its own fertility, maintain its own biological health, and exist in relationship with the rhythms of the natural world around it.

## The Core Practices

Biodynamic farming shares many practices with high-quality organic farming, but takes them further in specific ways. Here's what's central to the approach.

**Composting as a foundation.** Just as Albert Howard's Indore method was the heart of his soil improvement work, composting is absolutely central to biodynamic practice. Biodynamic compost is made with specific preparations — herbal compounds introduced at particular stages — that are meant to direct the biological processes and increase the vitality and diversity of the microbial community. Set aside the cosmological justifications for a moment: the result is high-quality, biologically active compost. That part is straightforward soil science.

**Integration of animals.** Biodynamic farms are meant to include animals as part of the biological cycle. The manures from livestock provide the primary organic matter input. The animals graze, cycle nutrients, and contribute to the biological diversity of the farm ecosystem. This closed-loop thinking — inputs and outputs cycling within the farm rather than being imported and exported — is something Gabe Brown also emphasizes strongly in his regenerative approach.

**Cover crops and crop rotation.** To build soil fertility continuously and prevent the monoculture trap, biodynamic farming uses diverse crop rotations and maintains cover crops between growing seasons. The goal is that living roots are always present in the soil, constantly feeding the biology through root exudates and decaying plant material. This is regenerative practice through and through.

**No synthetic inputs.** Biodynamic farming is certified organic, and then some. Not just no synthetic pesticides and fertilizers, but an active commitment to building the biological community that makes those inputs unnecessary. The reasoning is the same as Howard's: a healthy plant in healthy soil doesn't need chemical protection.

## The Biodynamic Preparations

This is the part that's distinctive to biodynamics and that raises the most questions from conventionally minded people. Steiner prescribed nine specific preparations — numbered 500 through 508 — made from specific medicinal herbs and mineral compounds processed in specific ways.

Preparation 500 is the most famous. It's made by packing fresh cow manure into a cow horn and burying it in the soil over winter. After six months, the horn is dug up and the transformed material inside is stirred into water in a specific way — alternating directions for an hour — and then sprayed on the soil. Biodynamic practitioners say this preparation stimulates microbial activity and root development in powerful ways.

The scientific community has had a mixed relationship with these preparations. Some studies have found measurable effects on soil biology and plant development. Others haven't replicated those findings. What's clear is that the preparations are used at very low concentrations, more as inoculants or activators than as direct nutrient inputs.

Honestly, my own view is that the preparation practices are less important than the larger biological orientation they come from. The farms producing the best results under biodynamic certification are doing so because of the composting, the crop diversity, the animal integration, and the no-till approach — not solely because of the horn manure. But if the preparation practices help farmers stay focused on the biological life of their soil, they're doing something right regardless of the mechanism.

## The Biodynamic Calendar

Steiner also promoted the idea of planting and harvesting according to a calendar based on lunar cycles and planetary positions. This was developed into a practical planting calendar by German farmer Maria Thun, who experimented with planting the same crops on different days and claimed to find consistent differences in growth and quality.

The calendar divides days into root, flower, leaf, and fruit days based on the position of the moon relative to constellations. Root crops like carrots and beets are best harvested on root days. Leafy greens on leaf days. Fruits and grains on fruit days.

Again, the scientific evidence for this specific practice is not robust. But traditional farmers throughout history have used lunar calendars for planting, and there's nothing inherently harmful about paying closer attention to natural rhythms. Some experienced biodynamic growers swear by the calendar. Others treat it more loosely. The practice is optional to good biodynamic outcomes.

## Real-World Examples of Biodynamic Farming

As of 2022, biodynamic techniques were being used on over 255,000 hectares in 65 countries. Germany, Italy, and France lead in adoption.

In wine, biodynamics has become prominent. Domaine Leroy in Burgundy, Movia in Slovenia, and many other high-end wine producers have adopted biodynamic certification, partly because they found that biodynamically managed vineyards produced grapes with more distinctive regional character — what wine people call

— than conventionally managed vineyards on the same soils. Some of the most celebrated and expensive wines in the world now come from biodynamic estates.

Marian Farms in California is a certified biodynamic operation growing almonds, pomegranates, and other tree crops on their land. They use all the core practices — preparations, animal integration, composting, minimal disturbance — and have documented significant improvements in soil biology over time.

In Texas, where I operate, the regenerative agriculture movement doesn't always fly under the biodynamic flag specifically, but the underlying practices overlap substantially. Messina Regenerative, where I've been contributing compost feedstocks, is doing the core biological work — composting at scale, building soil organic matter, eliminating synthetic inputs — that is biodynamics without the certification.

## Why This Matters for Home Gardeners

You don't need to bury cow horns in your backyard to get the benefits of biodynamic thinking. What you need is the orientation: the farm — or the garden — as a living organism that you're working with, not a machine you're operating.

That means composting. That means covering your soil and keeping it covered. That means adding diversity to your planting instead of growing the same things in the same spots year after year. That means feeding the biology and letting the biology feed your plants, instead of trying to short-circuit the system with synthetic inputs.

Albert Howard and Rudolf Steiner came at the same fundamental problem from very different directions. Howard was a scientist who studied traditional farming and built an empirical case for biological soil management. Steiner was a philosopher who intuited a holistic approach and built a practical system around it. They arrived at surprisingly similar conclusions: the soil is alive, the farm is an organism, and human health depends on keeping that biological system intact.

Y'all, biodynamics is a little bit mystical in places. But the core of it — biology first, chemicals never, the farm as a living thing — is exactly right. And that part doesn't require a cow horn to understand.
